item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the few articles that genuinely made the terrified. The fact that in-universe all it takes to forget us is (probably) a single click of a button is something that really makes you think. Awesome stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but this one just falls flat for me.

The fact that all the individual components of 2000 can be replicated in a lab setting ruins any chance for mystery. It's why the 055 + 579 deus ex machina is so good in my opinion, because the mechanisms behind it are completely mysterious, and left up to the reader's imagination.

This one is just boring, although the note at the end does save it a little. Also loads of technobabble is lame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The point of it is to be usable, though, so I don't think it's necessary. We can let the Foundation have nice things

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't we change "2020" to "2025" or something like that? SCP-2000 being presumably fixed and ready to go makes it a genuine Deus Ex Machina, and I don't really think that the "Please behave children, this is not a toy" note from the Administrator diminishes the fact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hurray! 2020 is so shitty that we might as well activate SCP-2000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is incredibly well written and definitely deserves my upvote. However, still not sure if I'm gonna take this into my personal headcanon as it kind of harms the purpose of the SCP universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1. really should have been a -001.
It's not just bad because it kills death in a way, it's bad because it kills normalcy. SCP articles are scary because they are supposed to exist in the real world (or an analogue thereof), as if there's this massive unknown anomalous world that exists alongside our normal one where things can and do go very very wrong, in ways that shouldn't be. Take for example that SCP with the submarine and the blood mist and all that. http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-1861. It's scary because it takes our established reality, and screws with it. It makes the people not quite human, it makes the world not quite ours.
But with SCP-2000 being a full-blown, mainlist SCP, which are supposed to be "more canon" (so to speak), instead of a possible origin story or "most important secret of the foundation", it kills normalcy.

If humans are already the weird and crazy monster to begin with, it makes something that transforms you that less creepy. If society is already some weird pre-progammed whatsit, why should a society changing SCP matter all that much?
SCP-2000 takes you out of the narrative by making shifting the baseline from normal to weird, and as such it kills a lot of what makes this wiki and its narratives great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welcome to the January 2020! SCP-2000 become normal now! Happy new year!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HOW DID I MISS THIS???? GAH ARE ARTICLES POPPING UP IN PREVIOUS SCP SPOTS AS I READ THROUGH GAHHHHH AT THIS RATE IMMA HAVE TO REREAD THE ENTIRE DATABASE. It's Ironic I find this one right after I finish scp-3000

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think of the tremendous effort that has gone into hiding the existence of this thing, even from high-ranking Foundation personnel. This thing is so dangerous to everything that it might as well be a cognitohazard in and of itself. "How many times have we been reset" is a horrible hole to crawl down into, and… nope. I'm not even going there.

That being said, if the Foundation just started manufacturing D-class personnel from this machine, eventually someone would notice. That someone would look into it and eventually report it to an "innocent" 04. That person would dig for a bit, maybe get a few other 04s in on the search, and then it's a toss up as to whether or not the 04s would be offed by the SCP2000 memetic kill agent or news would get to someone who has the appropriate clearance first. Due to the nature of SCP2000, the list of people "in the know" is shockingly small, so more than likely we'd end up with a bunch of important people dead for reasons no one could adequately explain.

That would prompt another investigation which would be quickly shut down by the 05s, but I'm betting that the risk would be so high that mandatory amnestics would have to be required Foundation-wide.

That would almost certainly alert at least one of the GoI spies, and can you imagine what kind of shitshow there would be if a Karcist got their hands on this place? It'd be a fleshcrafter's wet dream to have an endless supply of human meat to play with.

What about MC&D? Or the GoC? Or… or…

The scenarios are endless, so I'm gonna stick with Gene R's answer:

IMHO that is more practical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IMHO that is more practical.
From an ethical standpoint, a body manufactured by SCP-2000 and infused with someone's consciousness is the same as a real person. And drafting criminals into Class D means that fewer criminals return to the streets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So…is none going to comment on the fact that the Foundation has a litteral human-making machine? And not one or two, but 500.000 of them….I mean come on! I like this article as much as the next guy, but isn't this a bit too conventient? A machine that makes humans?

Oh.

Wait.

Then why do they continue using D-class if they can make their own testing subjects??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if that is just a cover?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's no secret that Yellowstone National Park is a volcano that is overdue for an eruption. What happens to this skip if the park blows it's lid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yellowstone park is the last hope for humanity. It's the last place anyone would expect.
Well written and well deserved as the winner of the 2000 entry competition.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
its not really contained in any way from what I understand
its just hidden from the outer world

I would say that keeping something hidden is an essential part of containment. Not everything needs to be transported to a Foundation site. Just making sure that it is hidden and monitored by the Foundation is effective for containing big structures or similar things.

Check out articles with the "structure" tag to see other SCPs that are buildings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't even anomalous how is it an SCP? and its a building how can it be contained
its not really contained in any way from what I understand
its just hidden from the outer world

item.title: Consistently Great
item.content:encoded:
Every time I see this one referenced I end up reading the entire thing. I only vaguely remember the 2000 contest it was written for but I always remember this one. The author definitely did some homework, and it shows. It reads like a believable technical document without being unpalatably dry, which suspends much of the creeping horror in the subtext with just enough notes scattered around to give you a peak and let you know it's there. It's extremely powerful and some have complained that it ruined the suspense in their head-canon (which is a poor argument), but it has clear technical limitations/weaknesses (even evident in that it was protected even more esoteric tech). It isn't a cosmic reset, its a last-ditch retreat and it seems to COST us every time. It isn't guaranteed. The idea that we might HAVE to use it scary, that we might not always be ABLE to also pretty damn scary.

I have (unfortunately) shied away from posting much on the site even though I've been around forever, but this one demands praise. I really think of it as classic and defining for the way I see the Foundation, and its one of the only ones I can remember solely by its number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At-least twice, probably more if the end note is to be believed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favorite part of this is the fact that it has been activated twice already. Really adds to the mystery of the whole scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know there are people who don't like this. Some people have said that this should have been a 001 proposal. This was one of the first Thaumiel class SCPs that wasn't a 001 and was done extremely well.

I also love the implications. It uses Foundation tech, but they have no idea where SCP-2000 came from. There is of course the implication that it has been used many times in the past. Finally, other SCPs and tales have shown that it isn't just a reset button as some people have criticized it.

item.title: Oddly enough, most disturbing thing here.
item.content:encoded: SCP-2000 is a massive machine that's slowly failing, and has been in operation for centuries, if not millennia. It also acts as our only hope is everything goes to shit. For me…. It makes you think, especially, at the end there.

item.title: Re: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: Uh, you’re replying to a comment made 4 years ago.

item.title: Re: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: It's just a cognitohazard, since it scans for consciousness and also mentions revival and amnestic administration.

item.title: Re: Scranton Box Ranges
item.content:encoded:
I was considering that too, but if that's the case I think it needs to be stated explicitly. The statement contradicts scp2000 using a larger model, and the two statements aren't being contrasted within the article (opposite ends of a long article).

I think the most likely explanation is it being an oversight.

item.title: Re: Scranton Box Ranges
item.content:encoded: It's saying that SRAs are so expensive, everywhere else in the Foundation besides 2000 is only allowed to use the little 2 m3 ones.

item.title: Scranton Box Ranges
item.content:encoded:
I loved this.
I think I've noticed an error with the Scranton devices, though.
They are arranged in a hexagon around the facility every 20 meters (meaning they are 20 meters from the facility, and each other, thanks to geometry).
The range on a Scranton device is said to be limited to 2 meters.
So, 20 meters away in 6 directions, there's a 2 meter reality anchored zone. I don't see how this helps anything.

Relevant quotes from article
Quotes

"The exterior surface of SCP-2000 is surrounded by Scranton Reality Anchors (SRAs) every 20 m, arranged hexagonally… "

"… Foundation-wide implementation of the device has been limited to units capable of an area of effect less than two cubic meters[1]."

item.title: Kinda hard to read
item.content:encoded:
But once you got there
You will find out how good is this is
Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, has this ever become so damaged that it couldn't operate? Because if that is the case, if an XK or other kind of scenario happens, they won't be able to bring the population back for a long time.

I assume everything would just be done for in that case, and humanity would truly become extinct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fix & reboot delaying many times…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read "BZHR" as 'brazier' (not the garment which is spelled "brassiere"). If intentional this was a nice wordplay; I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really like this kind of FOUNDATION MAGIC DEVICE
Ha

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pseudo-Riemannian_manifold

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One question I have about this article is what is a Pseudo-Riemannian manifold.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I was going to suggest 1422, incident zero since it’s a tale series centered on 2000, and my own 3480 since it’s caused by 2000 and FFB really liked it.

I heavily disagree with the 3001 link since the two articles are in opposing canons with regards to who Scranton is and how the anchors were created. 2000’s Scranton did his thing in the 19th century, while 3001 takes place in the 21st. Aside from the commonality of the name, they 100% contradict each other.

edit: for suggested links to the other two:

We can only suspend God’s disbelief so many times before the universe just says “no”.

and

During containment breach of SCP-████ on ██/██/████.2 , SCP-2000 experienced failure of several SRA and XACTS components which coincided with activation of the BZHR units on site.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I believe that the SRA was originally inspired by the "Scranton Box" from SCP-1451. Though I do encourage the SCP-3001 link,1 I do feel like SCP-1451 also fits.

In addition, Document Recovered from the Marianas Trench could possibly also be linked here. Maybe towards the end?

Footnotes
1. Withdrawing my support from this one because of Erasmus' post.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. I'm going to try and keep these simple and in-line links. The article mentions that it is located under Yellowstone, and I've seen it mentioned on a few occasions that in many headcanons this is the reason for SCP-1422. See no reason why a simple in-line link there would go awry. Additionally, the article mentions Robert Scranton, inventor of the SRA. Well, we now have an SCP article all about that particularly esteemed scientist, via SCP-3001. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

The entrance to SCP-2000 is disguised as a disused Park Ranger station in Yellowstone National Park. Despite several civilian trespassing attempts, the entrance has yet to be breached in the installation’s recorded history, and no further physical containment has been deemed necessary. Protocol Plainsight-201 is in effect for SCP-2000. Necessary supplies and replacement personnel may be delivered via unmarked road vehicles or civilian helicopter as appropriate.

To this:

The entrance to SCP-2000 is disguised as a disused Park Ranger station in Yellowstone National Park. Despite several civilian trespassing attempts, the entrance has yet to be breached in the installation’s recorded history, and no further physical containment has been deemed necessary. Protocol Plainsight-201 is in effect for SCP-2000. Necessary supplies and replacement personnel may be delivered via unmarked road vehicles or civilian helicopter as appropriate.

Changing this:

The invention of the Scranton Reality Anchor (SRA) appears to pre-date the first activation of SCP-2000, and is credited to Dr. Robert Scranton in 1889. The main body and much of the circuitry of the SRA are constructed of a corrosion resistant beryllium bronze alloy. Inspired by artifacts recovered [DATA EXPUNGED], effectively eliminating the appearance of virtual particle/anti-particle pairs required for Type Green reality bending phenomena to manifest. Due to the expense involved in producing the beryllium bronze alloy required for the SRA’s construction, Foundation-wide implementation of the device has been limited to units capable of an area of effect less than two cubic meters

To this:

The invention of the Scranton Reality Anchor (SRA) appears to pre-date the first activation of SCP-2000, and is credited to Dr. Robert Scranton in 1889. The main body and much of the circuitry of the SRA are constructed of a corrosion resistant beryllium bronze alloy. Inspired by artifacts recovered [DATA EXPUNGED], effectively eliminating the appearance of virtual particle/anti-particle pairs required for Type Green reality bending phenomena to manifest. Due to the expense involved in producing the beryllium bronze alloy required for the SRA’s construction, Foundation-wide implementation of the device has been limited to units capable of an area of effect less than two cubic meters

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still do not like this, and I still have never understood why it is so lauded. I recognize its influence and can appreciate what other works have done with it (SCP-3002 does it wonderfully), but this was done before by Dr. Gears, and done better.

The best I can say is that it does a great job of tying in with the wider lore of the Foundation universe, and its use of SCP-1422 is one of the best examples of crosslinking on the site. But the core premise isn't really ambitious, just well-executed.

Evidently the majority of people think I'm wrong, and this isn't going anywhere, but there will always be dissenting opinions on an acclaimed work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some very… harrowing implications in this article. I like it.

This is one of those skips that took the definition of not just skips, but the entire canon of the wiki, and turned it on its head. How this uses technical language to create one of the first narratives on the wiki, and how awe-inspiring it is, is simply amazing to me.

Great job on reaching +1000. This skip deserves all those upvotes and a thousand more.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So i saw a couple comments mentioning Clef and how the hell they keep him from finding out, can someone please explain that to me? I'm not very familiar as to how Dr. Alto Clef fits in the SCP story.

item.title: Wooh +1000
item.content:encoded: Congrats FortuneFavorsBold! One of the best and most influential works on this site finally cracked into 4 digits. Well deserved :)

The first Thaumiel to reach that much!
Love it; it's the best to use when something goes wrong, whether accidentally or otherwise…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love the article for how well it's written, also has anyone else mentioned the invisible sentence at the beginning?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I really like the concept, it feels like there's a crucial step from the reboot missing: Dealing with the anomalies that caused the K-class event to begin with.

In the event that using SCP-2000 has become necessary, the entire world has been sufficiently ruined by anomalies that the continued existence of the human race is questionable at best. However, I'm not seeing any plan for actually reclaiming the planet from these anomalies in the document. It goes straight from initiation of the Ganymede Protocol to rebuilding the planet.

As a secondary and much more minor concern, the functions of this device would throw off the common archaeological trick of dating historical records by analyzing any described astronomical phenomenon.
Here's to another 100, because this deserves every single one it has.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to be honest, I REALLY dislike this article as it stands. There are just way too many plot holes with it, and while each individually is fairly small, when you've got enough holes in something it turns into a sieve.

Critique
– hide block

The technology involved in this is absolutely titanic. Like, this SCP alone is sufficient technology - especially because of the explicit statement that it's all understood - to provide its own setting of POWERFULLY advanced sci-fi compared to anything I've seen indicated of the Foundation so far. As has been pointed out, it also really undercuts the entire POINT of having a population that DOESN'T know about the anomalies. Just…make people aware, or condition Foundation loyalty, and the entire job gets MUCH, MUCH easier and more effective.

Even if they are activating it to hide the Foundation again, why the emphasis on any specific year? Has the Foundation ALWAYS had sufficient ability/resources to ape the specific year they want? Leaves me confused and wanting more information, but not in the right way.

Also, as Dr Pelligrino mentions above(because this is one issue I hadn't seen on my first read).
> ◾Despite SCP-2000's prior activations those entities who would have survived an event that would necessitate the use of 2000 have either not hinted at their knowledge or such divulgence has been edited out of their SCP files and the memories of staff suppressed in the usual manner.

I cannot for a SECOND believe this one. I just can't. Nothing suggests the Foundation has the ability to perform these kinds of acts on this kind of scale ON THE ENTITIES THAT THEY CONTAIN, any number of which would HAVE to survive these events(682 much?). There's no way to contain this completely - amnesticizing the staff would require, in some situations, DAILY OR MORE OFTEN dosing of multiple staff members. I just cannot swallow this.

That said, and while this does continue to be a downvote for me, the writing is spectacular, the technobabble is incredibly believable and approachable, I really like the specific but subtle usage of existing Senior Staff, and the amount of implication woven into the article is incredibly adept. The writing itself is just phenomenally skilled.

I could maybe have gotten behind this one as a 001 proposal - the slight lack of consistency, the potential misinformation, and the individually world-defining nature of it could actually fly there - but it's just too full of internal problems for me to accept it on the mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe they use the cloning devices outside of SCP-2000 for the D-class making?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I'm guessing creating an unlimited reserve of Class D personnel would not be an acceptable use of this thing?

item.title: Recap/Summary
item.content:encoded:
So, to recap, just to get this thing straight in my head and, if I'm right, help other people that also had trouble figuring out this thing.

+ Spoilers, possibly, maybe.
- You were warned!
  • SCP-2000 is the Foundation's last resort for a near extinction event that leaves the earth habitable.
  • When activated, it clones and conditions a backup of humanity which is to rebuild before being forced to forget that the whole thing happened.
  • It functions using non-anomalous scientific principals and all subsystems have been reproduced in a lab.
  • The history of SCP-2000 has been lost or deliberately destroyed by previous iterations of the Foundation. The estimated age of SCP-2000, while blacked out, contains three (3) digits and we can presume from the inclusion of the SRAs (invented in 1889) that SCP-2000 was built in the ensuing years.
  • SCP-2000 has been used in the past an absolute minimum of two times as shown by the note written by Dr. Henrietta Eisenhower. One might also note the odd format used in the dates for addenda 1 and 2, wherein the years have an added ".2". Whether this implies that the current iteration of the Foundation has activated SCP-2000 once(calling their rediscovery of 2000 year".1"), twice(calling it year".0"), or not at all(merely keeping track of the amount they are certain of) is unclear.
  • Despite SCP-2000's prior activations those entities who would have survived an event that would necessitate the use of 2000 have either not hinted at their knowledge or such divulgence has been edited out of their SCP files and the memories of staff suppressed in the usual manner.
  • The activation of SCP-2000 has been used as an opportunity to decrease violent and sociopathic tendencies in humanity, greater action in this course was said to risk "noticeably inhibited" technological progress.
  • At some point prior to 2008, SCP-2000 activated due to an unknown cause and produced 10 million non-viable humanoids which expired five weeks later. These were distinct from modern humans, and were not subject to the usual implantation of memories.
  • After this previously mentioned incident, SCP-2000 has not returned to normal functions, current projections are that normal function will resume by 2020. However, the intervals between previously projected years, 2008, 2013, and 2020, show an upward trend. This may suggest that the Foundation's efforts are proving futile due to the Foundation's lower level of advancement or due to the efforts of an outside influence.
  • An outside party appears to have edited SCP-2000's file despite the security measures, which include a cogitohazardous image. This implies that this outside party was inoculated against the image or was immune for another reason.

The last resort is broken or compromised, next time it's used we have no guarantee that it will work or that what comes out will be human.

I think that about covers it.

+ SCP-2000’s maximum number of activations
- hide

Using the previous information about the absolute maximum age(999 years), the presence of the SRAs, and the note that it takes 25-50 years to rebuild human society to 2000CE we can calculate that SCP-2000 has been activated no more than 35 times assuming a constant cycle of disaster and rebuilding with only 124 years of progression. The Foundation has likely NOT failed in its mission that many times or in such a well ordered cycle, I simply posit that it would be patiently absurd to assume more.

I think that about covers it.

Pretty awesome in my opinion, though I didn't really understand the horror aspect until I took the time to take notes for this post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Christ, I think you people just don't get it! And, you know what? I don't think you ever will!

item.title: + all 1's
item.content:encoded: Best I've read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god, I just read the new hidden text, I love it even more now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took me a while to realize:

A Deus Ex Machina is a convenience in a story that solves any conflict at hand. It can go from a magical ability that comes out of nowhere, a sudden power outburst, or just a time reset.

Clever fuck, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed; especially with the new hidden text, the article seems to wholeheartedly support this perspective. 2000 doesn't save humanity from all those XK scenarios, it just forces another copy of humanity into existence and makes them live in another world made of thinly veiled "normalcy."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed that quite a few people are complaining about how this SCP removes the threat factor from other SCPs, and thus removes any sense of danger. However, considering the sheer number of "going to kill everything sometime in the future and theres nothing you can do about it hahaha" scp's out there, 2000 is a nice balance.

Happened to read this comment much later since I was re-reading this SCP for other reasons.

I find this SCP terrifying, and it's because of the second addendum. The first addendum is necessary to hammer in the fact that this isn't a real safety net in any sense. The second addendum hammers in how scary this thing really is.

This SCP doesn't remove the threat factor from other SCPs that are going to erase everything you know and love and there's nothing you can do to stop it. This SCP is one of those SCPs.

item.title: Re: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: A memetic kill agent is really a type of cognitohazard, since you need to perceive it for it to harm you.

item.title: Re: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: I don't know if this is correct or not, but shouldn't "cognitohazard" really be "memetic kill agent"?
